TYPES OF
OBJECTIONS
1. 	INTERNAL 	-­‐ you say these to yourself
2. 	EXTERNAL 	-­‐ customer says these to you
3. 	VALID 	-­‐ this is the objec[on
4. 	VALID BUT 	-­‐ this is the objec[on but won’t keep buyer from closing
5. 	INVALID 	-­‐ not the real objec[on
6. 	SPOKEN 	-­‐ you have heard the objec[on could be valid or invalid
7. 	UNSPOKEN 	-­‐ you have not heard the actual objec[on

DRILL FOR
HANDLING
OBJECTIONS
Listen
Do not interrupt, listen to duplicate. Nod your head.
Acknowledge
I hear you, I understand and actually I agree with you.
Isolate
Is that your only objec[on? What else would keep you from moving
forward? Do you have anything else you are concerned about?
Validate
That wouldn’t keep you from owning would it?
Determine Type
Determine what the objec[on is about product, price, you, a stall, valid,
invalid, spoken, unspoken.
Close Or Handle
Can you close or do you need to handle?

FIRST THINGS
FIRST
The first thing I do when I hear what appears to be an
objec[on is treat it like a complaint un[l further validated
as a real objec[on.
The first rule of selling will serve you well in determining
whether an objec[on is valid or not! If it is a legi[mate
objec[on, you will know soon enough because when you
try to handle as only a complaint, your prospect will make it
clear that this is more than that.
Trust me, most of what you hear in the close is not
objec[ons. They are just mere comments or complaints no
different than someone commen[ng on the weather.

-- 33 of 56 --

YOU CREATE YOUR
OWN OBJECTIONS
To explain to you what I mean by this I want to tell you a story
about one of my most trusted associates who once came to me
and said, “I can’t get any of my leads to close. It’s weird. They have
the money, they can do it, they want to, they can afford it and they
know it’s only a ma]er of [me before they will do it, but I just can’t
get them to close. I can’t figure it out. What do you think it is?”
I said, “Well it’s simple! You do the exact same thing in your life.
You won’t spend money on those things that you know you need
so when it happens to you in the close, you can’t close your
prospects because you agree with what they are saying! You need
to open the floodgates and start saying, ‘Yes!’ in your own life.
Your customers somehow reflect that exact belief (barrier) you
have and won’t release the money (just like you won’t).”
Quit asking customers to do what you won’t do.
© 2016. Cardone Training Technologies, Inc. All rights reserved. 	34
www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

USE THE
OBJECTION
TO CLOSE

longer term as a way to reduce monthly cost and
invalidate term as an objec[on.

GENERAL
OBJECTION
EXAMPLES
1. 	Don’t Know What I Want -­‐ If you did know, what do you think it would
be?
2. 	Don’t Know What I Want #2 -­‐ I understand. What are you sure you
don’t want? If you start backwards and figure out what they don’t want,
you have a chance of finding what they do want.
3. 	Don’t Know What I Want #3 -­‐ Excellent. What brought you out today to
look around?
4. 	A Customer Just Says To You “Give Me Your Card” -­‐ Give them the card.
If you have any ques[ons can I follow along so I can assist you in case
any ques[ons pop up? Mind if I tag along to answer ques[ons?
5. 	How Much Down -­‐ I’d like to show you with no money down. How
would that work for you?

RULES OF HANDLING
OBJECTIONS IN THE
NEGOTIATIONS
1. 	Never give any one objec[on too much power.
2. 	Never talk while the buyer objects.
3. 	Never take an objec[on personally.
4. 	Never handle more than one objec[on at a [me.
5. 	Never make verbal solu[ons or offers.
6. 	Never abandon nego[a[ons because of an objec[on.
7. 	Never disagree with the buyer.
8. 	Never confuse the person with the objec[on.
9. 	Never believe objec[on will keep the buyer from buying.
10. 	Never let the buyer get into a no-­‐win situa[on.
11. 	Never let the objec[on get you down.
12. 	Never ignore an objec[on.
13. 	Never quit communica[ng no ma]er what.
14. 	Never blame the customer or other condi[ons for the objec[on.
